question: What is the difference between a process and a thread? Give at least 4 differences.
idealAnswer: 1) Definition: A process is an independent program in execution with its own memory space. A thread is a lightweight unit of execution within a process. 2) Memory: Processes have separate memory (code, data, heap, stack). Threads share the same memory space (code, data, heap) but have separate stacks. 3) Creation: Process creation is heavyweight (requires OS intervention, memory allocation). Thread creation is lightweight. 4) Communication: Inter-process communication (IPC) is complex (pipes, sockets, shared memory). Threads communicate easily via shared variables. 5) Crash impact: If one process crashes, others are unaffected. If one thread crashes, the entire process may crash. 6) Context switching is faster for threads than processes.

question: Explain the Round Robin CPU scheduling algorithm. What is the effect of time quantum on performance?
idealAnswer: Round Robin assigns each process a fixed time slice (quantum). Processes execute for the quantum duration, then are moved to the back of the ready queue. It's preemptive and designed for time-sharing systems. Effect of time quantum: Too small quantum → too many context switches, high overhead, CPU spends more time switching than executing. Too large quantum → degenerates to FCFS (First Come First Served), long processes monopolize CPU. Optimal quantum → typically 10-100 milliseconds, should be large enough to handle most CPU bursts. Advantages: Fair, no starvation. Disadvantages: Higher average waiting time than SJF, context switch overhead.

question: What are the four necessary conditions for a deadlock to occur? How can you prevent deadlock?
idealAnswer: Four Coffman conditions (ALL must hold simultaneously): 1) Mutual Exclusion: At least one resource is non-shareable (only one process can use it at a time). 2) Hold and Wait: A process holding resources is waiting for additional resources. 3) No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a process. 4) Circular Wait: A circular chain of processes, each waiting for a resource held by the next. Prevention (break any one condition): 1) Eliminate Hold and Wait: Request all resources upfront. 2) Allow Preemption: Force release of resources. 3) Break Circular Wait: Impose ordering on resource requests. Detection: Use Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) — cycle indicates deadlock. Recovery: Kill a process or preempt resources.

question: What is virtual memory? How does it work? What are its advantages?
idealAnswer: Virtual memory is a memory management technique that gives each process the illusion of having its own large, contiguous address space, even if physical RAM is limited. How it works: The OS divides virtual memory into pages and physical memory into frames. A page table maps virtual pages to physical frames. When a process accesses a page not in RAM, a page fault occurs — the OS loads the page from disk (swap space) into a free frame. Advantages: 1) Programs can be larger than physical RAM. 2) Process isolation — each process has its own virtual space. 3) No need for contiguous allocation. 4) Sharing — multiple processes can share code pages. Disadvantage: Page faults cause significant slowdown (disk I/O).

question: Compare paging and segmentation in memory management.
idealAnswer: Paging: Divides memory into fixed-size blocks (pages/frames). No external fragmentation. May have internal fragmentation (last page partially filled). Invisible to programmer. Page table maps pages to frames. Simple to implement. Segmentation: Divides memory into variable-size segments based on logical divisions (code, data, stack). Each segment has a base and limit. External fragmentation possible. Visible to programmer (logical concept). Segment table maps segments. Paging: Physical division, fixed size, internal fragmentation. Segmentation: Logical division, variable size, external fragmentation. Modern systems use paged segmentation — combining both approaches.
keyPoints: paging: internal frag, segmentation: external frag
paging is physical, segmentation is logical

question: Compare FCFS, SJF, and Priority scheduling algorithms with their advantages and disadvantages.
idealAnswer: FCFS (First Come First Served): Non-preemptive. Processes execute in arrival order. Simple. Disadvantage: Convoy effect — short processes wait behind long ones. Not optimal for average wait time. SJF (Shortest Job First): Non-preemptive. Process with shortest burst time runs first. Optimal average waiting time. Disadvantage: Starvation of long processes, requires knowing burst time in advance (impractical). Priority Scheduling: Each process has a priority. Highest priority runs first. Can be preemptive or non-preemptive. Disadvantage: Starvation of low-priority processes. Solution: Aging — gradually increase priority of waiting processes. In practice, multilevel feedback queues are used, combining elements of all three.
keyPoints: SJF optimal avg wait but starvation

question: What is a semaphore? Explain the difference between binary semaphore and counting semaphore.
idealAnswer: A semaphore is a synchronization primitive used to control access to shared resources in concurrent programming. It has an integer value and two atomic operations: wait(P) — decrements value, blocks if value becomes negative. signal(V) — increments value, unblocks a waiting process. Binary Semaphore: Value is 0 or 1. Acts like a lock/mutex. Used for mutual exclusion — only one process in critical section. Example: protecting a shared variable. Counting Semaphore: Value can be any non-negative integer. Used to control access to a resource with limited instances. Value represents available resources. Example: limiting concurrent database connections to 10.

question: What are the different states of a process? Draw and explain the process state transition diagram.
idealAnswer: Five main process states: 1) New: Process is being created. 2) Ready: Process is loaded in memory, waiting for CPU. 3) Running: Process is executing on CPU. 4) Waiting (Blocked): Process is waiting for I/O or event. 5) Terminated: Process has finished execution. Transitions: New → Ready (admitted to ready queue). Ready → Running (scheduler dispatches/selects process). Running → Ready (preempted by scheduler, time quantum expired). Running → Waiting (process requests I/O). Waiting → Ready (I/O completed). Running → Terminated (process completes or is killed). Only one process is Running per CPU core at a time.
keyPoints: five states: new ready running waiting terminated
only one running per CPU core

question: What is the Producer-Consumer problem? How would you solve it using semaphores?
idealAnswer:
The Producer-Consumer (Bounded Buffer) problem: A producer produces items and puts them in a fixed-size buffer. A consumer removes items from the buffer. Problems: Producer must wait if buffer is full. Consumer must wait if buffer is empty. Both must not access the buffer simultaneously. Solution using semaphores:
```
semaphore mutex = 1;     // mutual exclusion for buffer
semaphore empty = N;     // count of empty slots (initially N)
semaphore full = 0;      // count of full slots (initially 0)

Producer:
    wait(empty);         // wait for empty slot
    wait(mutex);         // enter critical section
    add item to buffer;
    signal(mutex);       // exit critical section
    signal(full);        // increment full count

Consumer:
    wait(full);          // wait for full slot
    wait(mutex);         // enter critical section
    remove item from buffer;
    signal(mutex);       // exit critical section
    signal(empty);       // increment empty count
```

question: What is a page fault? What happens when a page fault occurs?
idealAnswer: A page fault occurs when a process tries to access a page that is in its virtual address space but not currently loaded in physical memory (RAM). Steps when page fault occurs: 1) CPU generates a trap to OS. 2) OS checks if the reference is valid (in the process's address space). If invalid → terminate process. 3) OS finds a free frame in physical memory. If no free frame → use page replacement algorithm (FIFO, LRU, Optimal) to evict a page. 4) If evicted page is dirty (modified), write it to disk first. 5) Load the required page from disk into the free frame. 6) Update the page table. 7) Restart the instruction that caused the fault. Page faults are expensive because disk I/O is ~1000x slower than RAM access.

question: What is a context switch? Why is it considered overhead?
idealAnswer: A context switch is the mechanism where the OS saves the state (context) of a running process and loads the state of another process to start executing it. The context includes: program counter, CPU registers, memory maps, process state, I/O status. Steps: 1) Save context of current process to its PCB (Process Control Block). 2) Update PCB state (running → ready/waiting). 3) Select next process (by scheduler). 4) Load context of selected process from its PCB. 5) Update PCB state (ready → running). 6) Resume execution. It's overhead because: The CPU does no useful work during the switch. Typical time: 1-10 microseconds. Cache and TLB may need to be flushed, causing cache misses after the switch. Frequent context switches degrade performance.
keyPoints: CPU does no useful work during switch

question: What is a file system? Explain different file allocation methods.
idealAnswer: A file system organizes how data is stored and retrieved on disk. It manages files, directories, and metadata. Three file allocation methods: 1) Contiguous Allocation: Each file occupies contiguous disk blocks. Fast sequential and random access. Problem: external fragmentation, difficult to grow files. 2) Linked Allocation: Each block contains a pointer to the next block (linked list). No external fragmentation, files can grow easily. Problem: slow random access (must traverse links), pointers use space. FAT (File Allocation Table) improves this by keeping links in a separate table. 3) Indexed Allocation: An index block contains pointers to all file blocks. Supports random access, no external fragmentation. Problem: index block overhead, large files may need multi-level indices. Unix/Linux uses inode-based indexed allocation.
keyPoints: linked = no frag but slow random access
indexed = best of both using index block

question: Explain the Banker's Algorithm for deadlock avoidance. How does it work?
hints: Think of a bank that must ensure it can satisfy all customers
idealAnswer: Banker's Algorithm decides whether to grant a resource request by simulating the allocation and checking if the system remains in a safe state. A safe state means there exists at least one sequence (safe sequence) in which all processes can complete. Data structures: Available[] — available resources. Max[][] — maximum resource needs per process. Allocation[][] — currently allocated. Need[][] = Max - Allocation. Algorithm: When a process requests resources: 1) Check if request ≤ Need (valid request). 2) Check if request ≤ Available (resources available). 3) Pretend to allocate. 4) Run safety algorithm — check if a safe sequence exists. 5) If safe → grant request. If unsafe → process must wait. Named 'Banker's' because a banker won't loan money unless they can guarantee all customers can be satisfied.
keyPoints: grant only if system stays safe

question: Compare the page replacement algorithms: FIFO, LRU, and Optimal.
idealAnswer: When a page fault occurs and no free frame is available, a page must be replaced. FIFO (First In First Out): Replace the oldest page in memory. Simple queue implementation. Problem: Belady's Anomaly — more frames can cause MORE page faults. LRU (Least Recently Used): Replace the page that hasn't been used for the longest time. Good practical performance. Can be implemented with counters or stack. More complex and hardware-expensive. Optimal (Belady's Algorithm): Replace the page that won't be used for the longest time in the future. Theoretical best (fewest faults). Impossible to implement in practice (requires future knowledge). Used as a benchmark. Ranking (best to worst): Optimal > LRU > FIFO. In practice, LRU approximations are used (clock algorithm).

question: What is a mutex? How is it different from a semaphore?
idealAnswer: A mutex (Mutual Exclusion) is a locking mechanism used to ensure only one thread can access a shared resource at a time. It has two states: locked and unlocked. Only the thread that locked the mutex can unlock it (ownership). Differences: 1) Ownership: Mutex has owner (only locker can unlock). Semaphore has no ownership (any thread can signal). 2) Purpose: Mutex is for mutual exclusion only. Semaphore can also be used for signaling/synchronization. 3) Value: Mutex is binary (0 or 1). Counting semaphore can have any positive value. 4) Priority inversion: Mutex supports priority inheritance to prevent priority inversion. Semaphore doesn't. Use mutex when you need simple locking. Use semaphore when you need to control access to N instances of a resource.
keyPoints: mutex for exclusion, semaphore also for signaling

question: What is an orphan process and a zombie process? How are they handled?
idealAnswer: Orphan Process: A process whose parent has terminated before it. The orphan is adopted by the init process (PID 1, or systemd). Init becomes the new parent and collects the zombie's exit status when the orphan terminates. Not harmful — handled automatically by the OS. Zombie Process: A process that has completed execution but still has an entry in the process table because the parent hasn't called wait() to read its exit status. Shows as 'Z' in ps output. Takes up a process table entry (PID) but no other resources. Too many zombies can exhaust process table slots. Fix: Parent should call wait()/waitpid(). Kill the parent (zombies are then adopted by init, which cleans them up).
keyPoints: orphan = parent died, adopted by init
zombie = finished but parent hasn't called wait

question: What is the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation?
idealAnswer: Internal Fragmentation: Wasted space INSIDE allocated memory blocks. Happens when allocated memory is larger than what the process needs. Example: Process needs 18KB but is given a 20KB block — 2KB wasted inside. Occurs in: fixed-size partitioning, paging (last page partially filled). External Fragmentation: Wasted space BETWEEN allocated memory blocks. Total free memory is enough but not contiguous. Example: 3 free blocks of 10KB each (total 30KB) but can't allocate 25KB because they're not adjacent. Occurs in: dynamic/variable-size partitioning, segmentation. Solutions: External fragmentation → compaction (move processes to create contiguous space) or paging. Internal fragmentation → use smaller allocation units.

question:
Given the following processes, calculate the average waiting time using SJF (non-preemptive) scheduling:

Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time
P1      | 0            | 6
P2      | 1            | 4
P3      | 2            | 2
P4      | 3            | 3
hints: SJF selects shortest job available at each decision point
idealAnswer:
SJF selects the process with shortest burst time among those that have arrived.

Time 0: Only P1 has arrived. P1 starts. Runs 0-6.
Time 6: P2(4), P3(2), P4(3) all arrived. Shortest = P3(2). Runs 6-8.
Time 8: P2(4), P4(3) available. Shortest = P4(3). Runs 8-11.
Time 11: P2(4) runs. Runs 11-15.

Gantt: |P1(0-6)|P3(6-8)|P4(8-11)|P2(11-15)|

Waiting time = Start time - Arrival time
P1: 0-0 = 0
P3: 6-2 = 4
P4: 8-3 = 5
P2: 11-1 = 10

Average waiting time = (0+10+4+5)/4 = 19/4 = 4.75

question: What is a system call? Give examples of system calls for process management and file management.
idealAnswer: A system call is the interface between a user program and the operating system kernel. Programs use system calls to request services from the OS (since user programs can't directly access hardware/kernel). Process management system calls: fork() — create new process. exec() — replace process image. wait() — wait for child to finish. exit() — terminate process. getpid() — get process ID. File management system calls: open() — open a file. read() — read from file. write() — write to file. close() — close file. lseek() — move file pointer. How it works: Program triggers a software interrupt (trap), CPU switches to kernel mode, OS executes the requested service, returns result, CPU switches back to user mode.
keyPoints: file examples: open read write close

question: What is starvation? How is it different from deadlock?
idealAnswer: Starvation: A process is indefinitely denied the resources it needs because other processes are always given priority. The process is ABLE to run (not blocked in a cycle) but never gets the chance. Example: In priority scheduling, low-priority processes never execute if high-priority ones keep arriving. Deadlock: Two or more processes are permanently blocked, each waiting for a resource held by the other. NONE of the processes can proceed. Differences: 1) In starvation, processes can potentially proceed; in deadlock, they cannot. 2) Starvation can involve one process; deadlock needs at least two. 3) Starvation can be resolved by aging (increasing priority over time); deadlock requires breaking one of the four conditions. 4) Deadlock is a subset of resource contention but more severe.

question: What is an Operating System (OS)? What are its primary functions?
idealAnswer: An Operating System (OS) is system software that acts as an intermediary between computer hardware and the user/applications. It provides a convenient environment for programs to run safely and efficiently. Primary functions: 1) Process Management (scheduling, creation/termination, synchronization). 2) Memory Management (allocating RAM, virtual memory, paging). 3) File System Management (storage organization, directories, access control). 4) Device Management (handling I/O devices via drivers). 5) Security & Protection (user authentication, preventing unauthorized access). 6) User Interface (CLI or GUI). Examples: Windows, Linux, macOS, Android.
keyPoints: Intermediary between hardware and user software

question: What is the difference between a Program and a Process?
idealAnswer: Program: A passive entity stored on secondary storage (disk). It is an executable file containing a set of instructions. It takes up space on disk but uses no CPU or RAM. Process: An active entity. It is a program in execution. When a program is launched, the OS loads it into RAM and allocates resources (CPU time, registers, stack, heap, data section). A single program can spawn multiple independent processes (e.g., opening multiple windows of the same web browser creates multiple browser processes).
keyPoints: Process has CPU time and memory allocated
One program can become multiple processes

question: What is a Race Condition? What is a Critical Section?
idealAnswer: Race Condition: A situation where multiple processes or threads access and manipulate shared data concurrently, and the final outcome depends on the unpredictable execution order (the 'race' over who finishes last usually overwrites data). It leads to data inconsistency and unpredictable bugs. Critical Section: A specific segment of code where shared resources (variables, files, databases) are accessed. To prevent race conditions, we must ensure that when one process is executing in its critical section, no other process is allowed to execute in its critical section. This is called 'Mutual Exclusion'. We use synchronization tools like Mutexes and Semaphores to protect critical sections.
keyPoints: Race condition = concurrent access causes data inconsistency
Final outcome depends on execution timing
Critical section = code segment accessing shared data
Critical sections must be protected via mutual exclusion

question: What is Cache Memory? Why is it important?
idealAnswer: Cache is extremely fast, small-capacity memory located close to or inside the CPU. It acts as a buffer between the fast CPU and the slower main memory (RAM). Why it's important: CPUs execute instructions much faster than RAM can provide data. Cache stores frequently or recently accessed data and instructions. When the CPU needs data, it checks the cache first (Cache Hit). If found, it saves a lot of time. If not found (Cache Miss), it fetches from RAM and stores a copy in the cache. It relies on the Principle of Locality: Temporal locality (recently accessed items will likely be accessed again) and Spatial locality (items near recently accessed items will likely be accessed).
keyPoints: Fast buffer between CPU and RAM
Stores recently/frequently used data to speed up CPU
Cache Hit (found) vs Cache Miss (not found)
Relies on Spatial and Temporal Locality
